title: How mirror neurons shape empathy in trauma survivors
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
You feel too much. Or not enough.

That's what trauma does to the mirror system. It's not a glitch. It's a survival adaptation.

Here's the thing. I spent years thinking something was broken in me. I'd walk into a room and feel everyone's mood like it was my own. My body would tense before anyone spoke. I'd absorb anger, grief, fear from people who hadn't even opened their mouths. And then I'd feel guilty for feeling what I felt, because I couldn't tell where I ended and they began.

Sound familiar?

Mirror neurons are the brain cells that fire both when you do something and when you watch someone else do it. They were discovered in the 1990s by a team led by Giacomo Rizzolatti at the University of Parma. These cells are part of how we understand each other. They help us feel what others feel. They're the biological basis for empathy.

But for trauma survivors, that system doesn't work the way it's supposed to. It works too hard. Or it shuts down completely.

Stay with me here. Because understanding this can change how you see your own reactions.

The hyper-empathic trap

I see this pattern constantly in the survivors I talk to. You're the person everyone comes to with their problems. You feel things before people say them. You walk into a room and immediately scan for danger, for mood shifts, for anything that might signal a threat.

This isn't a gift. It's a survival strategy that outlived its usefulness.

When you grow up in an unpredictable environment, your mirror neuron system learns to work overtime. You need to know what the parent is feeling before they act. You need to anticipate their mood to protect yourself. Your nervous system becomes a hyper-vigilant radar, constantly scanning for emotional data.

Let me tell you something that took me years to understand. The brain doesn't store trauma the way it stores your grocery list. It doesn't file it neatly in the hippocampus with a date stamp and a narrative arc. Trauma gets fragmented. Scattered across neural networks like shrapnel. A sound here. A body sensation there. A feeling with no story attached to it.

Bessel van der Kolk's research showed this clearly - traumatic memories live in the body, in the right hemisphere, in the amygdala. They bypass the language centers entirely. That's why you can't "think" your way out of a flashback. That's why someone can tell you "you're safe now" and your body doesn't believe a word of it.

Notice what's happening in your body right now. Is there tightness somewhere? A heaviness? Maybe a numbness that feels like nothing but is actually something. Your body has been holding this story for you. It's been doing that job faithfully, even when it cost you everything.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about this in his work. He describes how trauma survivors often lose the ability to feel their own internal signals because they're so focused on reading everyone else. Your interoception - your ability to sense your own body - gets hijacked by exteroception - reading the outside world.

Think about it. When was the last time you checked in with your own body without first checking on someone else's mood?

The cost is enormous. You feel exhausted because you're processing emotional data that isn't yours. You feel responsible for other people's feelings. You feel guilty when you can't fix someone's pain. And underneath all of that, you feel invisible because no one is reading you the way you read them.

Wild, right? The very system designed to connect us can leave us feeling completely alone.

The other side: empathy shutdown

But here's what I don't hear talked about enough. Some trauma survivors don't feel too much. They feel too little.

The mirror neuron system can also go the other direction. When the overwhelm becomes too great, the brain says no more. It shuts down the empathic response entirely. You stop feeling what others feel because feeling anything at all is too dangerous.

This is where I see people getting labeled as cold, selfish, or narcissistic. And that label can be devastating. Because the person who's shut down isn't choosing to be that way. Their nervous system has made a decision for them. It's a protective freeze response applied to empathy.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has written extensively about how trauma affects the brain's ability to process emotional information. He found that trauma survivors often have reduced activity in the medial prefrontal cortex, an area involved in self-awareness and empathy. The brain literally downregulates empathy to survive.

So here's the question I want you to sit with. Which pattern do you recognize in yourself? The one who feels everything and can't turn it off? Or the one who's learned to feel nothing because feeling is too painful?

Maybe you've been both at different times. I know I have.

Stay with me here. I know this can feel like a lot. The science, the terminology, the implications. But underneath all of it is something simple: your body has been trying to keep you alive. Every symptom you have - the anxiety, the dissociation, the hypervigilance, the numbness - those are survival strategies. They worked. They got you here.

Pat Ogden's sensorimotor psychotherapy approach is built on this understanding. The body isn't the enemy. It's not something to override or push through. It's the primary pathway back to yourself. When we listen to what the body is doing - really listen, without judgment - we start to understand the logic of our own survival.

Right now, as you read this, can you notice your feet on the ground? Can you feel the weight of your body in the chair? That's not a small thing. That's your nervous system practicing presence. Practicing being here, in this moment, instead of back there in the memory. Every time you do that - every single time - you're building new neural pathways. You're proving to your body that now is different from then.

There's no shame in either response. They're both adaptations. They both kept you alive. But they're also both keeping you stuck now.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Gabor Mate's work explains how chronic stress and suppressed emotion literally shape our biology. It's not an easy read, but it's an honest one.

Repairing the mirror

So how do you fix a mirror neuron system that's been bent out of shape by trauma?

First, you stop trying to fix it. You start understanding it.

Repair doesn't mean turning you into someone who feels the "right" amount of empathy. It means helping your nervous system find a middle ground. A place where you can feel connection without being consumed by it. Where you can sense others' emotions without losing yourself in them.

Here's what I've found works, and I want to be direct about this. It's not quick. It's not pretty. But it's real.

1. Body-based grounding. When you notice yourself absorbing someone else's emotion, bring your attention to your own physical sensations. Feel your feet on the floor. Notice your breath moving in and out. This isn't about escaping the other person. It's about reminding your brain that you have a body of your own. That you exist separately.

2. Time delay. The mirror neuron system works in milliseconds. You feel something before you can think about it. So create a gap. When you feel that pull to respond to someone's emotion, wait. Count to three. Take a breath. Let the initial impulse pass. Then decide what you actually want to do.

3. Permission to not feel. This one is hard. Give yourself permission to not feel what someone else is feeling. You are not required to match their emotional state to be a good person. You can be present with someone in pain without taking that pain into your body.

Something I want to name explicitly. If you're reading this and thinking "this doesn't apply to me because my trauma wasn't bad enough" - I hear you. And I want to challenge that thought gently. Trauma isn't measured by what happened to you. It's measured by what happened inside you. Two people can experience the same event and have completely different nervous system responses. Neither is wrong. Neither is "too sensitive." Your response was your response. It was real. It counts.

Gabor Mate says it perfectly: "Trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you." So if your body carries the imprint of something - if your nervous system is dysregulated, if your relationships are affected, if your sense of self was shaped by it - then it matters. Regardless of how it looks from the outside. Regardless of whether anyone else would call it "trauma." Your body doesn't lie.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana's work on polyvagal theory gives concrete exercises for regulating your system. It's the kind of book you can actually use, not just read.

4. Resourcing. Build up positive experiences in your body. This sounds simple, but it's not. When you feel safe, really feel it. Notice what that feels like in your hands, your chest, your stomach. The more you practice feeling safety, the more your nervous system learns that safety is possible. And the less you'll need to hyper-scan for danger.

What empathy actually is

Let me say something that might ruffle some feathers.

The wellness industry has sold us a version of empathy that's actually harmful for trauma survivors. They tell you to be open, to feel deeply, to for everyone. But they don't tell you that this can be retraumatizing for someone whose boundaries were never respected.

Real empathy doesn't require you to lose yourself. Real empathy is the ability to understand someone's experience while maintaining your own separate center. It's connection without fusion. It's compassion without collapse.

Stephen Porges, the creator of polyvagal theory, talks about this in terms of neuroception. Your nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety and danger. When your mirror neuron system is working in a regulated way, you can sense someone's emotion and still feel safe in your own body. You don't have to join them in their dysregulation.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). Deb Dana's clinical guide shows how to apply these concepts in real life. It's dense, but worth it.

So here's what I want you to take from this. Your empathy isn't broken. It's adapted. It's been shaped by the environments you survived. And that adaptation can change.

You don't have to be the person who feels everything. You don't have to be the person who feels nothing. You get to find your own middle ground. Your own way of connecting that doesn't cost you yourself.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It's an amino acid that promotes relaxation without sedation. Some people find it helps take the edge off the hyper-vigilance. It's not a solution, but it's a support.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read this. Maybe some tension released. Maybe you felt a little less alone. Maybe you felt resistance. All of that is information. Your nervous system is talking to you. The question is whether you're ready to listen.
